title: Whakamārama i te Horopaki Ahurea: Unpacking Cultural Context through the Story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: Exploring the cultural significance and pūrākau (stories) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) and its connection to Ngāti Kahungunu identity, history, and kaitiakitanga practices
learningObjectives: Analyze how language features and text structures in pūrākau convey cultural values and perspectives of Ngāti Kahungunu
Create written responses that demonstrate understanding of the relationship between Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and local cultural identity
Use oral language skills to share insights about the cultural significance of local landmarks with peers and community members
Apply critical thinking to compare traditional Māori narratives with contemporary representations of the awa in local media

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Pūrākau: Listening to the Stories
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua share pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, focusing on language features, imagery, and cultural values expressed. They create visual mind maps connecting key elements of the stories to cultural concepts like whakapapa, kaitiakitanga, and mana whenua.

activities.title: He Aha te Tikanga? Unpacking Cultural Meaning
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze excerpts from recorded pūrākau and local texts about the awa, identifying specific vocabulary, metaphors, and narrative structures that convey cultural meaning. They create glossaries of te reo Māori terms and their cultural significance.

activities.title: Tuhituhi mō te Awa: Writing with Cultural Understanding
activities.description: Students compose creative pieces (poems, short narratives, or descriptive essays) that reflect their understanding of the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating appropriate cultural references and demonstrating understanding of the relationship between place and identity.

activities.title: Kōrerorero: Sharing Perspectives
activities.description: Students participate in structured discussions and presentations where they share their written work and insights about the cultural context of the awa, using appropriate oral language conventions and demonstrating respect for cultural knowledge.

activities.title: Whakatairanga: Creating for Community
activities.description: Students collaborate to create digital or physical displays (such as story maps or cultural guides) that share their learning about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with the wider college community, demonstrating synthesis of cultural understanding and communication skills.

resources: Local kaumātua and knowledge holders from Ngāti Kahungunu
Recorded pūrākau and historical accounts of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku from the Mangakōtukutuku College archives
Digital mapping tools (Google Earth, ArcGIS) for exploring the physical and cultural landscape of the awa
'Ngā Pūrākau o Te Matau a Māui: Stories from Hawke's Bay' by local authors and historians

tasks: Create a written analysis of how language features in a pūrākau convey cultural values
Compose a creative piece that demonstrates understanding of the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Deliver an oral presentation sharing insights about the relationship between the awa and local identity

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways
